primary_diagnosis.name: Large cervical furuncle (cutaneous abscess) with surrounding cellulitis

pathophysiology_summary: A staphylococcal follicular infection on the nape of the neck has matured into a fluctuant furuncle (cutaneous abscess) with a pointing pustular tip and surrounding cellulitis, plus systemic features (fever, chills, malaise) and tender regional lymphadenopathy. The definitive treatment of a fluctuant, pointing abscess is incision and drainage; oral antibiotics (anti-staphylococcal, e.g., flucloxacillin) treat the surrounding cellulitis but do not substitute for drainage. Discriminators: no pre-existing painless lump or punctum argues against an infected epidermoid cyst; a single contiguous fluctuant mass rather than multiple draining sinuses distinguishes a furuncle from a carbuncle; superficial pointing with normal swallowing/voice/breathing argues against a deep neck-space infection — but the neck location mandates an explicit deep-space/airway screen and a sepsis screen. A telehealth clinician cannot perform I&D, so the correct action is urgent in-person drainage plus antibiotics.

differential.evidence_basis: Rapidly enlarging hot tender lump with a pointing pustular tip and systemic symptoms.
Can mimic an abscess, but there was no pre-existing painless lump or punctum.
Large lesion borders on carbuncle but remains a single mass without multiple sinuses.
Neck location mandates screening for dysphagia/drooling/voice/airway involvement.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Recognise a pointing abscess needing I&D (which cannot be done remotely), screen for sepsis and deep neck-space/airway involvement, and arrange urgent in-person care.

differential.evidence_basis: Fluctuant pointing abscess with cellulitis and mild systemic upset; swallowing/voice/breathing intact.
No dysphagia, drooling, voice change or airway compromise, with superficial pointing.
Working diagnosis: urgent in-person incision & drainage plus oral anti-staphylococcal antibiotics; sepsis/deep-neck safety-net.

red_flags.description: Systemic features (fever/chills/malaise) with expanding cellulitis — impending sepsis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Systemic toxicity signals risk of progression to sepsis, needing prompt drainage and antibiotics and possible admission.

red_flags.description: Deep neck-space/airway involvement: dysphagia, drooling, voice change, breathing difficulty
red_flags.clinical_significance: A neck infection extending to deep spaces is airway-threatening.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed deep neck-space infection can obstruct the airway or spread to the mediastinum.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Prescribing antibiotics alone for a fluctuant pointing abscess without arranging incision and drainage.
Telling the patient to squeeze or lance the abscess themselves.
Not screening for airway/swallowing involvement or systemic sepsis given the neck location.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Assessing regional lymphadenopathy and deep neck spaces
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent same-day in-person assessment for incision and drainage and oral anti-staphylococcal antibiotics; ED if systemic sepsis or any airway/swallowing involvement.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Treating with antibiotics alone (no drainage) or advising self-drainage; missing sepsis or a deep neck-space infection.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines: skin and soft tissue infections
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Fluctuant abscesses require incision and drainage; add oral anti-staphylococcal antibiotics (e.g., di/flucloxacillin) when there is surrounding cellulitis or systemic features.
